column8: “and/so if there should be a hundred”

column2: These readings represent linguistic options in using the feminine for the word mi'ah ('hundred'), or the masculine due to its referents (male fighters). The second reading combines these options [al-Mahdawi].

column8: “who cannot go the right way unless led”

column2: *Shuʿbah from ʿĀṣim actually has yihiddī, included here for simplification.
NB: Some pronounced this with an overshort vowel, closer to the third reading.
The above readings are various ways to realise form VIII from the same root, originally yahtadī.

column8: "who cannot guide, but must be led"

column2: This reading is based on form I of the root h-d-y. It may also be
Combined translation: “who cannot go the right way unless led"
These readings provide complementary meanings. It also possible that the last reading is another shortened form of yahtadī, hence the readings all mean the same [al-Mahdawi].

In this and the following reading, the first jussive verb is based on the root r-t-ʿ, meaning enjoyment or eating heartily. Both verbs are conjugated for the third person singular, as the brothers were referring to Joseph (pbuh).
In this reading, the verbs are conjugated for the first person plural, i.e. the brothers including Joseph.

In this and the following reading, the first jussive verb is based on the root r-ʿ-y, meaning to graze (as an expression for eating, or literally to send out one's animals to pasture). Here again, the pronouns are for Joseph.
In this reading, the verbs are conjugated for the brothers including Joseph.

column8:
“are the depths of darkness equal to the
 light?”

column2: These readings represent linguistic options for gender agreement with the broken plural [al-Mahdawi].
This is the form II verb in the active voice, conjugated for God with the first-person majestic plural.
This is the same form II verb in the passive.
These two readings have the form V verb with a reflexive meaning, realised in two ways. NB: al-Bazzī has another narration agreeing with the majority pronunciation of this verb.
These readings amount to the same meaning [al-Mahdawi].
These two readings are forms IV and I of the same verb, with the same meaning, ascribed to God with the first-person majestic plural.
The form I verb is here ascribed to the cattle etc. which produce the milk by God's will. Masculine and feminine pronouns can be alternated for this kind of plural.

column8: “give me iron blocks...give me copper to pour”

column2: In this reading, both occurrences of the verbs are form IV, 'give'.

column8: “give me iron blocks...come to (help) me as I pour copper”

column2: In this reading, the first verb is form IV, 'give', and the second is form I, 'come' (hence the object qiṭran is acted upon only by the verb ufrigh).

column8: “bring me iron blocks...bring me copper to pour”

column2: In this reading, both occurrences of the verbs are form I, 'come', but there is an implied ba' particle, hence it means 'bring'. *When joining to the previous verse, this is pronounced radman-iʾtūnī. Another narration from Shuʿbah has the second word as ātūnī, hence 'come...give/bring'.
These readings amount to the same meaning [al-Alusi].
These readings represent linguistic options for this word and are identical in meaning [al-Mahdawi].
